author: Sofia Martínez
title: Google Ads vs Meta Ads: Which Platform Wins for Local Services
description: We break down platform strengths for service businesses. Google Ads captures intent-driven leads; Meta builds brand awareness. The real answer depends on your budget and growth stage.

sections.p: Every local service business owner we work with asks the same question: should I spend my ad budget on Google or Facebook? The answer isn't "one or the other"—it's understanding what each platform actually does and when to use it. We've managed $50K+ monthly across both platforms for HVAC contractors, plumbers, electricians, and cleaning services. The businesses that win don't pick sides. They allocate strategically based on what each platform excels at.

sections.h2: Google Ads: High Intent, Higher Cost, Immediate ROI

sections.p: Google Ads captures people actively searching for your service right now. Someone types "emergency plumber near me" or "furnace repair cost 2026"—that's a qualified lead already convinced they need help. We've seen service businesses achieve 3:1 to 5:1 return on ad spend with Google Search campaigns. But here's the reality: you're competing for every click with every other contractor in your market.

Cost per click for local services averages $15–$50 depending on your market and season. In competitive metros like Chicago or Denver, plumbing clicks hit $40–$75. You need solid conversion rates to justify spend. A locksmith paying $25 per click needs to book jobs at roughly 10–15% conversion to break even at $200 average service call value. Google Local Services Ads (the lead format, not PPC) typically perform better—we see 8–12% conversion rates and flat-fee-per-lead pricing ($5–$15). The trade-off: limited ad real estate, and Google controls lead quality.

list: Best for: Emergency services, repair businesses, high-intent seasonal demand (HVAC in summer, plumbing in winter)
Timeline to ROI: 2–4 weeks with optimized campaigns
Average CPC: $15–$50 in most markets; $40–$75 in competitive metros
Conversion rate benchmark: 5–12% depending on service category

sections.h2: Meta Ads: Awareness, Retargeting, Lower Cost

sections.p: Meta (Facebook/Instagram) reaches people who aren't actively searching for your service yet. A homeowner sees an Instagram ad for your cleaning service, clicks, and books 3 weeks later—they weren't typing "cleaners near me" that day. This changes your cost structure. Meta CPC typically runs $2–$8 for local services, roughly 75% cheaper than Google Search. But the conversion path is longer and conversion rates sit at 1–3% for cold audiences, compared to 5–12% on Google's high-intent traffic.

Meta excels at retargeting. We run campaigns where service businesses show ads only to people who visited their website or clicked a previous ad. Retargeting conversion rates hit 8–15% because you're warming up warm leads. For a $3K monthly budget: Google Search might book 3–4 jobs; Meta cold traffic might book 1–2 jobs but build brand awareness for 10,000 local users; Meta retargeting might book 4–6 jobs from people already interested.

sections.quote: We see the highest ROI when businesses use Meta for brand building and retargeting, then redirect warm traffic to Google Search campaigns. It's not either/or—it's a funnel.

list: Best for: Brand awareness, retargeting warm leads, service categories with longer decision cycles (roofing, solar, kitchen remodels)
Timeline to ROI: 6–8 weeks for cold audience campaigns; 2–3 weeks for retargeting
Average CPC: $2–$8 for cold audiences; lower with retargeting
Conversion rate benchmark: 1–3% cold traffic; 8–15% retargeting

sections.p: We recommend this split for service businesses under $50K monthly ad budget: 60% Google (Search + Local Services), 40% Meta (cold traffic + retargeting). If you're under $5K/month, go 100% Google Search—the intent match is too strong to leave on the table. Once you scale past $10K/month, layer in Meta for volume and awareness. Above $30K/month, you have room to experiment with both platforms' full toolkits.

Here's a concrete example: A roofing company with $8K monthly budget allocated 70% to Google ($5,600) and 30% to Meta ($2,400). Google Search brought in 12 qualified leads at $467 CPC—they closed 3 jobs (25% close rate = $6K revenue per lead acquired). Meta cold traffic brought 8 clicks at low cost but only 1 conversion. But Meta retargeting of website visitors brought 4 more conversions at 3:1 ROAS. Total: 8 closed jobs, $48K revenue, 4.1:1 ad spend return.

sections.h2: When to Invest Heavily in Each Platform

list: Go heavy Google if: You're in an emergency/immediate-need category (plumbing, locksmith, electrical); you have a high close rate (15%+); seasonal demand is crucial to your revenue
Go heavy Meta if: Your service has a longer decision cycle (30–90 days); you're building brand awareness in a new market; you have a retargeting pixel installed and working
Use both equally if: You have $20K+ monthly budget; you want to test creative/messaging differences; you're competing in a saturated local market

sections.p: The mistake we see most: service businesses dump $5K into Google, see 8 leads, close 1 job, and quit. They don't give it enough runway or volume. Google needs scale—at least $2K/month in spend—to optimize properly. Meta's mistake is the opposite: businesses run $500 cold-traffic campaigns, get 2% conversion, and assume Meta doesn't work for them. Meta works for retargeting and brand building, not cold prospecting at small budget levels.
